speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 12, 1996. I'm the interviewer, Mark Goldberg, interviewing Chief Prosecutor William Dowdell Denson. We're in Lawrence, New York in the USA. The language is English. And this is tape number three. Perhaps we could go through each trial briefly on this tape. We'll start with the first trial at Dachau. Can you tell me a little bit about the trial and perhaps tell me what stands out after all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Court was appointed by General Lucian Truscott. He was known as a military government court and was composed of approximately nine members, one of whom was the president of court. And that position was held in all the cases usually by a general officer, that is, either a major general or brigadier general. The prosecution staff was also appointed by General Truscott with the aid of the war crimes branch of [? Yuspet ?] and of headquarters Third Army. And that same thing was true in respect to all four cases. When I say four cases, I mean Dachau, Mauthausen, Flossenburg, and Buchenwald. And the personnel of the courts were usually nine field grade officers. That meant that they were at the rank of major and above. And most of them were full colonels, men who had served approximately 25 to 30 years in the army and were men of experience. And their function here in this trial as members of the court were to act as fact [? miners, ?] like a jury would act. But it was more than that because they also participated in the determination of what sentence each one of their accused was to receive. And the defense counsel were those that were not only appointed by the court but also those that were selected by their accused. In other words, each accused in-- in every one of these trials had the right to be represented by a lawyer of his choice. And Uncle Sam footed the bill for the [INAUDIBLE] fees. And a lot of the accused were content to have those officers and men, who had been appointed as defense counsel, by the appointing authority to serve him in their defense. But they were free to have brought to the court any witnesses of their choosing to be heard in their behalf. In other words, it was recognized that these trials were being held by a victor nation. And we wanted it to be thoroughly understood that these accused were being given the same or substantially the same rights that a citizen of the United States would have who has been charged with the commission of a crime in the Federal Courts. And those guideposts prevailed in all four trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: What stands out about the trial at Dachau? Is there a particular person you prosecuted who stands out? Is there a particular incident that stands out or a particular piece of evidence that stands out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, basically that each trial was, while they were all concentration camps, there was differences. And the personnel that were brought to trial, they different just like day and night. So there was no uniformity in the sense that when you heard one you heard it all. Because each one of the camps, being operated by different groups of accused, different types of conduct prevailed in each one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in Dachau, for instance, what do you remember of Michael Redwoods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Michael Redwoods was, as I remember at this point, and he was an assistant Schutzhaftlagerfuhrer. Now, my German is terrible. I've got an Alabama accent. And I can't quite curl it over to speak the German that-- with the facility that probably I should. But Redwoods was-- he was really the second highest [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] officer in charge of Dachau. And being a Schutzhaftlagerfuhrer he carried out his duties as well prescribed. But he was unfortunately, like most of these accused, blood thirsty and mercy was not known to them. In Dachau, I prosecuted a man by the name of-- [LAUGHS] his name escapes from me at this moment-- Dr. Karl Klaus Shilling. Dr. Shilling was a noted authority in the field of medicine relating to tropical diseases. And his function in Dachau was to develop a specific for malaria, because quinine was hardly available due to the war that was being carried on in the Pacific.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Dr. Shilling's part was using-- in the operation of the camp-- was to use these prisoners as guinea pigs to further his work in experimenting upon developing a specific for malaria. Dr. Shilling was a man of advanced years. Being as old as I am now, he was younger than I am now, but I'm 83. And Shilling at the time he was in camp was in his late 60s or early 70s.

speaker: interviewee
text: He would take these prisoners and have them exposed to [INAUDIBLE] mosquitoes that had malaria. And he would take a little cage out of wire and strap it on to the five prisoners. Mosquito would feed on the prisoner for a while. And he would develop malaria. And then it was the doctor's job to try to develop something that would cure that malaria. Then during the course of his work, many, many prisoners died, because the path that he chose was to develop a-- a specific, which was mercurial in nature. And as a result of it, many of these prisoners died from kidney diseases that were developed as a result of their absorbing lots of mercurial substances. And during the examination of Dr. Shilling, it was the only thing that sort of shook me a little bit. Because here was a man of stature. And he got on the stand there and started lying about he-- his treatment of the prisoners and that he did not take a prisoner that was emaciated, as a guinea pig, but those who seemed to be physically able to serve as proper subjects. And unfortunately, that was a slight departure from the truth. He had subjected practically all types of prisoners, those who were sick and those who were well to his experiments. And many of them died. But when I was examining him on cross-examination, he denied that. Well, we had the records where the-- some of his subjects had-- even had typhus and were suffering from other diseases at the time, that they were exposed to Shilling's work. And when I pulled out the records, he says, oh, my god. I thought they had been burned. He had been told that the records of this work would never fall in the hands of anybody else. And of course, he broke down after confronted with the records and his people who were assisting him at camp. And he was distraught about the fact that he was exposed to doing things that a man of basic morality would have shunned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the second trial was at Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still lieutenant colonel when you did that trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of August Eigruber or Werner Grahn.

speaker: interviewee
text: August Eigruber. August Eigruber was without doubt the most obnoxious individual that I-- that I prosecuted. Smart, he was the-- one of two Gauleiters that ruled Austria. He was a Gauleiter. His [? job ?] I think was, I think, of Austria. They called [INAUDIBLE]. In any event, he was a man who, at that time, was about 45 years of age, which was quite young to hold a position that he held. Indeed, I had gotten him from Mr. Justice Jackson, who was a prosecutor at Nuremberg. Well, we had been comparing Mauthausen and had run into the name Eigruber and quite a few documents. And we tried to find him in our war crimes [? cage. ?] And he wasn't there. And we put out a search for him. And finally it turned up that he was in-- being held at Nuremberg. Well, I called Mr. Justice Jackson and told him if he could release Eigruber to me, I had a case there which would justify hanging him as high as Haman. And Mr. Justice Jackson said, well, that's pretty high. So go right ahead. We'll let you have him. And we had him. And he took his seat along with 60 other accused in Mauthausen. He was found guilty and was executed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What exactly were his crimes?

speaker: interviewee
text: His crimes were killing these prisoners [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who couldn't defend themselves at all, using these prisoners as guinea pigs. And as such, not-- well, it-- it's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think runs into not only morals that normal human beings are expected to have, but certainly was contrary to the medical profession to use persons that served as his guinea pigs and using them against that will.

speaker: interviewer
text: At any point, did he admit his guilt? Was he at least repentant that he resisted?

speaker: interviewee
text: These people didn't admit guilt. To them, there was never any guilt attached to their conduct. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many of them, or rather none of them, ever expressed contrition in any form. [? There was ?] [? never ?] sorry. They thought that what they had done was correct. And that applied to everybody from the camp kommandant on down to the lowliest kapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Eigruber was-- he-- his activity was expansive. He had a concentration camp of his own in the sense that he had prisoners that he utilized in carrying out the functions of his office as well. And he, Eigruber, had participated in executions of prisoners that were sent to Mauthausen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't think though that there was any evidence that he actually used his hands to beat anyone. But he had reported prisoners, that reports require their position of beatings as a punishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was Eigruber. And Eigruber, he was contemptuous of the court. And of course, any time I could examine him in an area that would exhibit that contempt, I did it. And he was [INAUDIBLE]. And I later found out from [? him, ?] he kept him. And when they put the noose around Eigruber's head, the last thing he did was say, heil Hitler! And he [INAUDIBLE]. No contrition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eigruber and others, Eigruber in this case was a ranking German officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you make of the concept of superior orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, superior--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that any sort of defense or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, superior orders really only applies when the order that was given by the superior is a lawful order. The operation of these camps were really against German law. They had laws against assault and battery. That was a crime on the German law. They had laws against taking the property of others. So that the operation of these camps were really in violation of German law, although they were authorized by Himmler and Himmler was certainly a man in authority. But the leaders cannot revoke and hold for naught the laws of the country. And superior orders only applied to those orders that are disobeyed that are lawful orders. There never was one shred of evidence where a man who disobeyed a lawful order was punished in any way-- in any way, maybe going a little far. There was a doctor by the name of Willy Yopps in Mauthausen, for example, who declined to inject some women who had been working in the bordello. And as such, had become pregnant. The rule was that you didn't have to work there unless you wanted to. But if you got pregnant, the price was death. And the death was administered by giving them shots of Salvarsan and mercury, or hydrogen peroxide, or even Lysol. Well, Willy Yopps had some of these women in-- in the hospital. And he declined to inject them. Well, Willy Yopps, we tried him later, not for that. But at that time when he refused that order, he was just transferred to another camp. Well, we tried him anyway. And he was found guilty. And I think he was-- I don't remember whether he was hanged or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just as an-- as a relevant aside, if there can be such a phrase, you said the women only had to work in the bordello if they were willing to. What was their option if they didn't work in the bordello?

speaker: interviewee
text: To remain as prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they doing forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got-- they got-- they got better food when they were in the bordello.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So it wasn't as if they were going to be freed or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Oh, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just went back to being prisoners, or treated as prisoners, perhaps forced labor or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What about Flossenburg? Is there some particular person who stands out in your mind at that trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flossenburg was a camp with 45 people who were tried in that case. I did not prepare Flossenburg. Flossenburg was prepared by a colonel who died before. But I think the case started on the 12th of June, on 1946. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Colonel [? Shaw ?] who was the chief prosecutor there, had a stroke and died. And I was asked to take over the trial. And that case lasted up until the middle of January of 1947. Because Colonel [? Shaw ?] had prepared it like they were 45 murder cases. And that five-- 45 murder cases successively took a much longer time than preparing a case under the common exam. So that Flossenburg was just as horrible a place as-- I think it was a Class II camp. The Germans had these camps classified. Dachau was a Class I. Bergen-Belsen was a Class II. Mauthausen was a Class III, otherwise known as an extermination center. And extermination centers were those that had gas chambers. Dachau's gas chamber didn't go in operation until the late '43, or '44. Mauthausen they leased-- and by the way, when I say leased, Eigruber was the Gauleiter at the time. And he leased Castle [INAUDIBLE] to the-- the [INAUDIBLE] department of concentration camp use. And they took out the basement and made that into a gas chamber. And that's at Mauthausen, as its base of operations so far as extermination [? at ?] Mauthausen was concerned. And Class IV was also a waste. And that-- I'm not so sure. But I think Buchenwald was in a Class IV.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any one prisoner who stands out at Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any one prisoner who stands out at Flossenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankly, all of them were such skunks that it was a poor choice between [? riding that horse. ?] But they had quite a impressive group of individuals there in prison. They had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some French generals-- officers-- that were there that were executed by the Germans. And Blum, I think, was there, the Frenchman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon Blum from France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Blum. Yeah. And I forgot now. Who is the head of Austria at the time of the Anschluss? Oh. I'm getting too old. I can't remember. But anyway, he and his wife were there. And they had-- well, in Dachau, we had Leopold, Frederick Leopold, Prince of Prussia. He was there, a prisoner. And who was the preacher?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Pastor Niemoller?

speaker: interviewee
text: Niemoller, Pastor Niemoller was at Dachau. The-- the-- what the krauts did, they'd overun the countries, they would take those individuals who either politically or financially were important, and who could say there's a nucleus for opposition. And they [? backed them-- ?] packed them up and sent them off to these camps. And Flossenburg had its share of-- of dignitaries, if you please. But Flossenburg was really famous for the-- sending its prisoners down to Dachau. The march from Flossenburg down to Dachau was terrible. And there were thousands and thousands of bodies that that just littered the way from-- from Flossenburg down to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is really what was known as the death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop here and continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.
